title: Work-Stealing Thread Pool
tagline: A from-scratch concurrent executor in C++
description: A lock-free-ish work-stealing scheduler with futures support, built for EECS 482 to deeply understand parallelism primitives.
problem: Understanding how real runtimes schedule parallel work requires going below the abstraction of `std::async` and OpenMP.
approach: Implemented per-worker deques with Chase-Lev work stealing, a global task queue for overflow, and a `future`/`promise` wrapper for ergonomic task submission.
solution: Near-linear speedup on CPU-bound benchmarks up to core count. Stress tests pass under ThreadSanitizer with no data races.
learned: False sharing and memory ordering bugs are subtle. Measuring before optimizing — a naive mutex pool was only 2× slower on 8 cores, which reframed where complexity pays off.
